html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Nine — Part Two

The Book of Tehillim

TEHILLIM

The Psalms — The Praises of Yisra'EL

Psalms 21 through 41

The Songs of Dawid — Book I Complete

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

11Q5 (11QPsa) — Cave 11 — Major Psalms Scroll — Ps 101-150 + Ps 151, 154, 155

4Q83 (4QPsa) — Cave 4 — Psalms 5-71 fragments

4Q85 (4QPsc) — Cave 4 — Psalms 16, 18, 27

4Q87 (4QPse) — Cave 4 — Psalms 76-88

4Q88 (4QPsf) — Cave 4 — Psalms 22, 107, 109

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — All 150 Psalms plus Psalm 151

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

BOOK I — PSALMS 21 THROUGH 41 — COMPLETE

TEHILLIM — PSALM 21 Of Dawid — The King Rejoices in YHWH's Strength

1 YHWH, in your strength the king rejoices — and in your salvation how greatly he exults.

2 You have given him his heart's desire and have not withheld the request of his lips.

3 For you meet him with rich blessings — you set a crown of fine gold upon his head.

4 He asked life of you — you gave it to him, length of days forever and ever.

5 His glory is great through your salvation — splendor and majesty you bestow on him.

6 For you make him most blessed forever — you make him glad with the joy of your presence.

7 For the king trusts in YHWH, and through the steadfast love of the Most High he shall not be moved.

8 Your hand will find out all your enemies — your right hand will find out those who hate you.

9 You will make them as a blazing oven when you appear — YHWH will swallow them up in his wrath, and fire will consume them.

10 You will destroy their descendants from the earth, and their offspring from among the children of man.

11 Though they plan evil against you, though they devise mischief, they will not succeed.

12 For you will put them to flight — you will aim at their faces with your bows.

13 Be exalted, YHWH, in your strength — we will sing and praise your power.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 21 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm is a royal thanksgiving — the king giving thanks for YHWH's answer to the prayer of Psalm 20. Together Psalms 20 and 21 form a liturgical pair: prayer before battle and thanksgiving after.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 21 in full. The crown of fine gold in verse 3 is read as the covenant symbol of royal legitimacy granted by YHWH rather than by human appointment.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 21. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — you make him glad with the joy of your presence. The presence of YHWH is itself the highest blessing — not the throne, not the crown, not the victory over enemies. The covenant king's ultimate joy is not in what YHWH gives but in who YHWH is. Verse 13 — be exalted, YHWH, in your strength — we will sing and praise your power. The psalm closes with the congregation joining the king's praise.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 22 Of Dawid — My Creator, My Creator, Why Have You Forsaken Me

1 My Creator, my Creator, why have you forsaken me — why are you so far from saving me, so far from my cries of anguish?

2 My Creator, I cry out by day but you do not answer, and by night, but I find no rest.

3 Yet you are holy, enthroned on the praises of Yisra'EL.

4 In you our fathers trusted — they trusted, and you delivered them.

5 To you they cried and were rescued — in you they trusted and were not put to shame.

6 But I am a worm and not a man, scorned by mankind and despised by the people.

7 All who see me mock me — they make mouths at me — they wag their heads.

8 He trusts in YHWH — let him deliver him — let him rescue him, for he delights in him.

9 Yet you are he who took me from the womb — you made me trust you at my mother's breasts.

10 On you was I cast from my birth, and from my mother's womb you have been my Creator.

11 Be not far from me, for trouble is near, and there is none to help.

12 Many bulls encompass me — strong bulls of Bashan surround me.

13 They open wide their mouths at me, like a ravening and roaring lion.

14 I am poured out like water, and all my bones are out of joint — my heart is like wax — it is melted within my breast.

15 My strength is dried up like a potsherd — and my tongue sticks to my jaws — you lay me in the dust of death.

16 For dogs encompass me — a company of evildoers encircles me — they have pierced my hands and feet.

17 I can count all my bones — they stare and gloat over me.

18 They divide my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots.

19 But you, YHWH, be not far off — O you my help, come quickly to my aid.

20 Deliver my soul from the sword, my precious life from the power of the dog.

21 Save me from the mouth of the lion — you have rescued me from the horns of the wild oxen.

22 I will tell of your name to my brothers — in the midst of the congregation I will praise you.

23 You who fear YHWH, praise him — all you offspring of Yaakov, glorify him — and stand in awe of him, all you offspring of Yisra'EL.

24 For he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one — he has not hidden his face from him — but has listened to his cry for help.

25 From you comes my praise in the great congregation — my vows I will perform before those who fear him.

26 The afflicted shall eat and be satisfied — those who seek him shall praise YHWH — may your hearts live forever.

27 All the ends of the earth shall remember and turn to YHWH — and all the families of the nations shall worship before you.

28 For kingship belongs to YHWH, and he rules over the nations.

29 All the prosperous of the earth eat and worship — before him shall bow all who go down to the dust, even the one who could not keep himself alive.

30 Posterity shall serve him — it shall be told of YHWH to the coming generation.

31 They shall come and proclaim his righteousness to a people yet unborn, that he has done it.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 22 is attested in 4QPsf (4Q88) from Cave 4 which preserves portions of this psalm. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions. Psalm 22 is one of the most extensively cited psalms in the ancient Jewish world — the opening cry and the description of suffering in verses 6-18 are among the most theologically significant passages in the Tehillim.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 22 in full. The psalm is read as the paradigmatic cry of the righteous sufferer who maintains covenant trust and moves from anguish to praise.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 22. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading in preserved portions.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 22 moves through three stages: desolation — verses 1-21 — the absence of YHWH felt completely while covenant history is remembered; transition — verse 21 — you have rescued me; and praise — verses 22-31 — the congregation and the nations drawn into testimony. Verse 24 is the theological hinge: he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one — he has not hidden his face — but has listened to his cry. The appearance of abandonment in verses 1-2 is answered here. YHWH was present and listening the entire time. Verses 27-31 expand the deliverance to cosmic scope — all the ends of the earth, all families of nations, posterity yet unborn.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 23 Of Dawid — YHWH Is My Shepherd

1 YHWH is my shepherd — I shall not want.

2 He makes me lie down in green pastures — he leads me beside still waters.

3 He restores my soul — he leads me in paths of righteousness for his name's sake.

4 Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for you are with me — your rod and your staff, they comfort me.

5 You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies — you anoint my head with oil — my cup overflows.

6 Surely goodness and steadfast love shall follow me all the days of my life, and I shall dwell in the house of YHWH forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 23 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in all preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 23 in full. The image of YHWH as shepherd — leading, restoring, protecting — is central to the Beta Israel covenant theology and connected to the Mosaic imagery of YHWH leading Yisra'EL through the wilderness.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 23. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 23 is six verses that contain the entire covenant relationship in concentrated form. Verse 1: provision — I shall not want. Verse 2: rest and guidance. Verse 3: restoration and righteousness — for his name's sake — the motivation is YHWH's own covenant character, not human merit. Verse 4: protection through the darkest valley — not the removal of the valley but the presence through it. Verse 5: honor before enemies. Verse 6: the ultimate covenant promise — I shall dwell in the house of YHWH forever. The psalm moves from the shepherd's field to the temple — from creation to covenant habitation.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 24 Of Dawid — The Earth Is YHWH's — The King of Glory Enters

1 The earth is YHWH's and the fullness thereof, the world and those who dwell therein,

2 For he has founded it upon the seas and established it upon the rivers.

3 Who shall ascend the hill of YHWH — and who shall stand in his holy place?

4 He who has clean hands and a pure heart, who does not lift up his soul to what is false and does not swear deceitfully.

5 He will receive blessing from YHWH and righteousness from the Creator of his salvation.

6 Such is the generation of those who seek him, who seek the face of the Creator of Yaakov.

7 Lift up your heads, O gates — and be lifted up, O ancient doors — that the King of Glory may come in.

8 Who is this King of Glory — YHWH, strong and mighty — YHWH, mighty in battle.

9 Lift up your heads, O gates — and lift them up, O ancient doors — that the King of Glory may come in.

10 Who is this King of Glory — YHWH of hosts — he is the King of Glory.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 24 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm has a liturgical structure — an entrance liturgy for the Ark of the Covenant entering the sanctuary. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 24 in full. The threefold declaration — the earth is YHWH's, the qualification for entering his presence, and the acclamation of the King of Glory — is read as the complete theology of covenant access.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 24. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 24 opens with the cosmic claim — the earth is YHWH's and the fullness thereof — before asking who may enter his presence. The answer in verse 4 — clean hands and a pure heart — echoes Psalm 15. The second half — verses 7-10 — is a processional liturgy for the Ark entering the gates. YHWH is the King of Glory — strong and mighty in battle — YHWH of hosts. The psalm connects creation sovereignty — verses 1-2 — with covenant access — verses 3-6 — with divine warrior kingship — verses 7-10. These three are inseparable in the ancient Hebrew covenant theology.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 25 Of Dawid — An Acrostic Prayer — Teach Me Your Ways

1 To you, YHWH, I lift up my soul.

2 O my Creator, in you I trust — let me not be put to shame — let not my enemies exult over me.

3 Indeed, none who wait for you shall be put to shame — they shall be ashamed who are wantonly treacherous.

4 Make me to know your ways, YHWH — teach me your paths.

5 Lead me in your truth and teach me, for you are the Creator of my salvation — for you I wait all the day long.

6 Remember your mercy, YHWH, and your steadfast love, for they have been from of old.

7 Remember not the sins of my youth or my transgressions — according to your steadfast love remember me, for the sake of your goodness, YHWH.

8 Good and upright is YHWH — therefore he instructs sinners in the way.

9 He leads the humble in what is right, and teaches the humble his way.

10 All the paths of YHWH are steadfast love and faithfulness, for those who keep his covenant and his testimonies.

11 For your name's sake, YHWH, pardon my guilt, for it is great.

12 Who is the man who fears YHWH — him will he instruct in the way that he should choose.

13 His soul shall abide in well-being, and his offspring shall inherit the land.

14 The friendship of YHWH is for those who fear him, and he makes known to them his covenant.

15 My eyes are ever toward YHWH, for he will pluck my feet out of the net.

16 Turn to me and be gracious to me, for I am lonely and afflicted.

17 The troubles of my heart are enlarged — bring me out of my distresses.

18 Consider my affliction and my trouble, and forgive all my sins.

19 Consider how many are my foes, and with what violent hatred they hate me.

20 Oh, guard my soul, and deliver me — let me not be put to shame, for I take refuge in you.

21 May integrity and uprightness preserve me, for I wait for you.

22 Redeem Yisra'EL, O Creator, out of all his troubles.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 25 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. It is an alphabetic acrostic. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 25 in full. Verse 14 — the friendship of YHWH is for those who fear him, and he makes known to them his covenant — is read as the essence of covenant intimacy.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 25. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 14 — the friendship of YHWH is for those who fear him, and he makes known to them his covenant. The Hebrew sod — translated friendship — means intimate counsel, the inner circle of shared confidence. YHWH has an inner circle of covenant relationship reserved for those who fear him. He discloses to them what he does not disclose to others. This connects to Amos 3:7 — YHWH does nothing without revealing his sod to his servants the prophets. The Qumran community understood their access to the hidden things — the raz nistarim — as precisely this sod relationship with YHWH.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 26 Of Dawid — Vindicate Me YHWH — I Have Walked in Integrity

1 Vindicate me, YHWH, for I have walked in my integrity, and I have trusted in YHWH without wavering.

2 Prove me, YHWH, and try me — test my heart and my mind.

3 For your steadfast love is before my eyes, and I walk in your faithfulness.

4 I do not sit with men of falsehood, nor do I consort with hypocrites.

5 I hate the assembly of evildoers, and I will not sit with the wicked.

6 I wash my hands in innocence and go around your altar, YHWH,

7 Proclaiming thanksgiving aloud, and telling all your wondrous deeds.

8 YHWH, I love the habitation of your house and the place where your glory dwells.

9 Do not sweep my soul away with sinners, nor my life with bloodthirsty men,

10 In whose hands are evil devices, and whose right hands are full of bribes.

11 But as for me, I shall walk in my integrity — redeem me, and be gracious to me.

12 My foot stands on level ground — in the great assembly I will bless YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 26 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm's separation from the wicked resonates strongly with the Qumran community's own self-understanding as a separated covenant remnant. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 26 in full. Verse 8 — YHWH, I love the habitation of your house and the place where your glory dwells — is read as the covenant person's orientation toward the sanctuary as the center of life.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 26. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 26 is a psalm of covenant integrity — the psalmist presenting himself before YHWH for examination. Verse 2 — prove me, YHWH, and try me — test my heart and my mind — is the invitation to divine scrutiny rather than the avoidance of it. The separation from evildoers in verses 4-5 is not self-righteousness — it is the maintenance of covenant identity. The Community Rule of the Qumran community — 1QS — requires the same separation: the Sons of Light do not walk in the ways of the Sons of Darkness.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 27 Of Dawid — YHWH Is My Light and My Salvation — One Thing I Ask

1 YHWH is my light and my salvation — whom shall I fear — YHWH is the stronghold of my life — of whom shall I be afraid?

2 When evildoers assail me to eat up my flesh, my adversaries and foes — it is they who stumble and fall.

3 Though an army encamp against me, my heart shall not fear — though war arise against me, yet I will be confident.

4 One thing have I asked of YHWH, that will I seek after — that I may dwell in the house of YHWH all the days of my life, to gaze upon the beauty of YHWH and to inquire in his temple.

5 For he will hide me in his shelter in the day of trouble — he will conceal me under the cover of his tent — he will lift me high upon a rock.

6 And now my head shall be lifted up above my enemies all around me, and I will offer in his tent sacrifices with shouts of joy — I will sing and make melody to YHWH.

7 Hear, YHWH, when I cry aloud — be gracious to me and answer me.

8 You have said, Seek my face — my heart says to you, Your face, YHWH, do I seek.

9 Hide not your face from me — turn not your servant away in anger — O you who have been my help. Cast me not off — forsake me not, O Creator of my salvation.

10 For my father and my mother have forsaken me, but YHWH will take me in.

11 Teach me your way, YHWH, and lead me on a level path because of my enemies.

12 Give me not up to the will of my adversaries, for false witnesses have risen against me, and they breathe out violence.

13 I believe that I shall look upon the goodness of YHWH in the land of the living.

14 Wait for YHWH — be strong, and let your heart take courage — wait for YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 27 is attested in 4QPsc (4Q85) which preserves portions of this psalm. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 27 in full. The psalm is read as the complete theology of covenant trust — confidence in YHWH's protection, personal prayer, and the declaration of faith and exhortation to wait.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 27. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — one thing have I asked of YHWH — that I may dwell in the house of YHWH all the days of my life, to gaze upon the beauty of YHWH. Of everything available to ask, the psalmist asks for one thing: presence with YHWH. Not victory, not health, not prosperity — presence. The beauty of YHWH — the Hebrew no'am — is the quality of YHWH's own being beheld in the sanctuary. Verse 14 — wait for YHWH — be strong — let your heart take courage — wait for YHWH. The double wait frames the command to be strong. Waiting is not passivity but active, courageous trust.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 28 Of Dawid — Do Not Be Silent — My Strength and My Shield

1 To you, YHWH, I call — my rock, be not deaf to me, lest, if you be silent to me, I become like those who go down to the pit.

2 Hear the voice of my pleas for mercy, when I cry to you for help, when I lift up my hands toward your most holy sanctuary.

3 Do not drag me off with the wicked, with the workers of evil, who speak peace with their neighbors while evil is in their hearts.

4 Give to them according to their work and according to the evil of their deeds — give to them according to the work of their hands — render them their due reward.

5 Because they do not regard the works of YHWH or the work of his hands, he will tear them down and build them up no more.

6 Blessed be YHWH — for he has heard the voice of my pleas for mercy.

7 YHWH is my strength and my shield — in him my heart trusts, and I am helped — my heart exults, and with my song I give thanks to him.

8 YHWH is the strength of his people — he is the saving refuge of his anointed.

9 Oh, save your people and bless your heritage — be their shepherd and carry them forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 28 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 28 in full. Verse 9 — save your people and bless your heritage — be their shepherd and carry them forever — is read as the communal covenant prayer expanding the individual petition to encompass all of YHWH's people.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 28. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — who speak peace with their neighbors while evil is in their hearts. This is the double heart condemned in Psalm 12:2. The covenant community's concern with integrity of speech runs throughout the Tehillim. The DSS Community Rule — 1QS — prohibits speaking to a fellow member while harboring a grievance in the heart. Psalm 28 language of inner evil concealed behind outer peace shapes the Qumran community's ethics of transparent speech.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 29 Of Dawid — The Voice of YHWH Over the Waters — The Thunderstorm Psalm

1 Ascribe to YHWH, O heavenly beings, ascribe to YHWH glory and strength.

2 Ascribe to YHWH the glory due his name — worship YHWH in the splendor of holiness.

3 The voice of YHWH is over the waters — the Creator of glory thunders — YHWH, over many waters.

4 The voice of YHWH is powerful — the voice of YHWH is full of majesty.

5 The voice of YHWH breaks the cedars — YHWH breaks the cedars of Lebanon.

6 He makes Lebanon to skip like a calf, and Sirion like a young wild ox.

7 The voice of YHWH flashes forth flames of fire.

8 The voice of YHWH shakes the wilderness — YHWH shakes the wilderness of Kadesh.

9 The voice of YHWH makes the deer give birth and strips the forests bare, and in his temple all cry, Glory.

10 YHWH sits enthroned over the flood — YHWH sits enthroned as king forever.

11 May YHWH give strength to his people — may YHWH bless his people with peace.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 29 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The sevenfold repetition of the voice of YHWH — kol YHWH — is one of the most distinctive structural features in the Tehillim, reflecting the seven-day covenant calendar. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 29 in full. The theophany of YHWH's voice over the waters is read in connection with the creation account of Bereshit 1 where YHWH speaks the creation into existence over the waters.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 29. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The voice of YHWH — kol YHWH — sounds seven times in verses 3-9. Seven is the number of covenant completeness throughout the Hebrew texts. The seven sounds of YHWH's voice in the storm are the covenant number declaring YHWH's complete sovereignty over the natural order. Verse 10 — YHWH sits enthroned over the flood — YHWH sits enthroned as king forever. The flood is tehom — the primordial deep — and also the mabul — the Flood of Noakh. YHWH rules over both the original chaos and the judgment flood.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 30 A Psalm of Dawid — Weeping for a Night, Joy in the Morning

1 I will extol you, YHWH, for you have drawn me up and have not let my foes rejoice over me.

2 YHWH my Creator, I cried to you for help, and you have healed me.

3 YHWH, you have brought up my soul from Sheol — restored me to life from among those who go down to the pit.

4 Sing praises to YHWH, O you his saints, and give thanks to his holy name.

5 For his anger is but for a moment, and his favor is for a lifetime — weeping may tarry for the night, but joy comes with the morning.

6 As for me, I said in my prosperity, I shall never be moved.

7 By your favor, YHWH, you made my mountain stand strong — you hid your face — I was dismayed.

8 To you, YHWH, I cry — and to YHWH I plead for mercy.

9 What profit is there in my death, if I go down to the pit — will the dust praise you — will it tell of your faithfulness?

10 Hear, YHWH, and be merciful to me — YHWH, be my helper.

11 You have turned for me my mourning into dancing — you have loosed my sackcloth and clothed me with gladness,

12 That my glory may sing your praise and not be silent — YHWH my Creator, I will give thanks to you forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 30 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions. The superscription connects the psalm to the dedication of the temple.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 30 in full. Verse 5 — weeping may tarry for the night, but joy comes with the morning — is one of the most cited verses in the Beta Israel tradition as a declaration of covenant hope through suffering.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 30. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — weeping may tarry for the night, but joy comes with the morning. The proportion is deliberately asymmetrical: a moment of anger, a lifetime of favor. The same covenant principle appears in Shemot 34:6-7 where YHWH declares himself slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love. Verse 11 — you have turned my mourning into dancing. The reversal is YHWH's active work — not time healing the wound but YHWH himself turning the mourning.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 31 Of Dawid — Into Your Hand I Commit My Spirit

1 In you, YHWH, do I take refuge — let me never be put to shame — in your righteousness deliver me.

2 Incline your ear to me — rescue me speedily — be a rock of refuge for me, a strong fortress to save me.

3 For you are my rock and my fortress — and for your name's sake you lead me and guide me.

4 You take me out of the net they have hidden for me, for you are my refuge.

5 Into your hand I commit my spirit — you have redeemed me, YHWH, faithful Creator.

6 I hate those who pay regard to worthless idols, but I trust in YHWH.

7 I will rejoice and be glad in your steadfast love, because you have seen my affliction — you have known the distress of my soul,

8 And you have not delivered me into the hand of the enemy — you have set my feet in a broad place.

9 Be gracious to me, YHWH, for I am in distress — my eye is wasted from grief — my soul and my body also.

10 For my life is spent with sorrow, and my years with sighing — my strength fails because of my iniquity, and my bones waste away.

11 Because of all my adversaries I have become a reproach, especially to my neighbors, and an object of dread to my acquaintances — those who see me in the street flee from me.

12 I have been forgotten like one who is dead — I have become like a broken vessel.

13 For I hear the whispering of many — terror on every side — as they scheme together against me, as they plot to take my life.

14 But I trust in you, YHWH — I say, You are my Creator.

15 My times are in your hand — rescue me from the hand of my enemies and from my persecutors.

16 Make your face shine on your servant — save me in your steadfast love.

17 YHWH, let me not be put to shame, for I call upon you — let the wicked be put to shame — let them go silently to Sheol.

18 Let the lying lips be mute, which speak insolently against the righteous in pride and contempt.

19 Oh, how abundant is your goodness, which you have stored up for those who fear you and worked for those who take refuge in you, in the sight of the children of mankind.

20 In the cover of your presence you hide them from the plots of men — you store them in your shelter from the strife of tongues.

21 Blessed be YHWH, for he has wondrously shown his steadfast love to me when I was in a besieged city.

22 I had said in my alarm, I am cut off from your sight — but you heard the voice of my pleas for mercy when I cried to you for help.

23 Love YHWH, all you his saints — YHWH preserves the faithful but abundantly repays the one who acts in pride.

24 Be strong, and let your heart take courage, all you who wait for YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 31 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. Verse 5 — into your hand I commit my spirit — is among the most significant verses in the entire DSS corpus, appearing in the context of the Qumran community's theology of dying in covenant faithfulness. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 31 in full. The psalm's movement from deep affliction through declared trust to communal exhortation is read as the covenant pattern of individual suffering becoming communal testimony.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 31. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — into your hand I commit my spirit — you have redeemed me, YHWH, faithful Creator. The act of committing the spirit into YHWH's hand is the ultimate act of covenant trust — the surrender of the most essential part of the self to YHWH's keeping. Verse 15 — my times are in your hand. Not merely the spirit at death but the entire flow of days and years are in YHWH's keeping. The enemy does not hold them. Circumstances do not hold them. YHWH holds them.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 32 Of Dawid — A Maskil — Blessed Is the One Whose Transgression Is Forgiven

1 Blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered.

2 Blessed is the man against whom YHWH counts no iniquity, and in whose spirit there is no deceit.

3 For when I kept silent, my bones wasted away through my groaning all day long.

4 For day and night your hand was heavy upon me — my strength was dried up as by the heat of summer.

5 I acknowledged my sin to you, and I did not cover my iniquity — I said, I will confess my transgressions to YHWH — and you forgave the iniquity of my sin.

6 Therefore let everyone who is godly offer prayer to you at a time when you may be found — surely in the rush of great waters, they shall not reach him.

7 You are a hiding place for me — you preserve me from trouble — you surround me with shouts of deliverance.

8 I will instruct you and teach you in the way you should go — I will counsel you with my eye upon you.

9 Be not like a horse or a mule, without understanding, which must be curbed with bit and bridle, or it will not stay near you.

10 Many are the sorrows of the wicked, but steadfast love surrounds the one who trusts in YHWH.

11 Be glad in YHWH, and rejoice, O righteous, and shout for joy, all you upright in heart.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 32 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions. Psalm 32 is the second of the traditional seven penitential psalms.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 32 in full. The psalm is read as the complete cycle of sin, concealment, physical consequence, confession, and forgiveness — the covenant pattern of restoration.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 32. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 3-5 describe the physical consequence of unconfessed sin — bones wasting away, groaning all day, strength dried up. The connection between moral and physical states is not incidental in the ancient Hebrew covenant framework. The body responds to the state of the covenant relationship. Silence — refusal to confess — is itself destructive. Verse 5 — I acknowledged my sin and you forgave. The covenant mechanism is simple: acknowledgment is met with forgiveness. What the human tries to cover by silence, YHWH covers by forgiveness when the human uncovers it by confession.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 33 Shout for Joy in YHWH — He Spoke and It Was Done

1 Shout for joy in YHWH, O you righteous — praise befits the upright.

2 Give thanks to YHWH with the lyre — make melody to him with the harp of ten strings.

3 Sing to him a new song — play skillfully on the strings, with loud shouts.

4 For the word of YHWH is upright, and all his work is done in faithfulness.

5 He loves righteousness and justice — the earth is full of the steadfast love of YHWH.

6 By the word of YHWH the heavens were made, and by the breath of his mouth all their host.

7 He gathers the waters of the sea as a heap — he puts the deeps in storehouses.

8 Let all the earth fear YHWH — let all the inhabitants of the world stand in awe of him.

9 For he spoke, and it came to be — he commanded, and it stood firm.

10 YHWH brings the counsel of the nations to nothing — he frustrates the plans of the peoples.

11 The counsel of YHWH stands forever, the plans of his heart to all generations.

12 Blessed is the nation whose Creator is YHWH, the people whom he has chosen as his heritage.

13 YHWH looks down from heaven — he sees all the children of man.

14 From where he sits enthroned he looks out on all the inhabitants of the earth,

15 He who fashions the hearts of them all and observes all their deeds.

16 The king is not saved by his great army — a warrior is not delivered by his great strength.

17 The war horse is a false hope for salvation, and by its great might it cannot rescue.

18 Behold, the eye of YHWH is on those who fear him, on those who hope in his steadfast love,

19 That he may deliver their soul from death and keep them alive in famine.

20 Our soul waits for YHWH — he is our help and our shield.

21 For our heart is glad in him, because we trust in his holy name.

22 Let your steadfast love, YHWH, be upon us, even as we hope in you.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 33 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm has no superscription. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 33 in full. Verse 6 — by the word of YHWH the heavens were made — is read as the explicit connection between the creation account of Bereshit 1 and the covenant sovereignty celebrated in Tehillim.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 33. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — he spoke, and it came to be — he commanded, and it stood firm. This is the theology of Bereshit 1 compressed into one verse. YHWH's word is creative — not descriptive or advisory but causative. Verse 11 — the counsel of YHWH stands forever, the plans of his heart to all generations. Against verse 10 — YHWH brings the counsel of nations to nothing — the permanence of YHWH's own counsel is declared. Human plans fail. Divine purposes endure. Verse 15 — he who fashions the hearts of them all. YHWH made the human heart. He therefore knows it completely.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 34 Of Dawid — An Acrostic — Taste and See That YHWH Is Good

1 I will bless YHWH at all times — his praise shall continually be in my mouth.

2 My soul makes its boast in YHWH — let the humble hear and be glad.

3 Oh, magnify YHWH with me, and let us exalt his name together.

4 I sought YHWH, and he answered me and delivered me from all my fears.

5 Those who look to him are radiant, and their faces shall never be ashamed.

6 This poor man cried, and YHWH heard him and saved him out of all his troubles.

7 The angel of YHWH encamps around those who fear him, and delivers them.

8 Oh, taste and see that YHWH is good — blessed is the man who takes refuge in him.

9 Oh, fear YHWH, you his saints, for those who fear him have no lack.

10 The young lions suffer want and hunger, but those who seek YHWH lack no good thing.

11 Come, O children, listen to me — I will teach you the fear of YHWH.

12 What man is there who desires life and loves many days, that he may see good?

13 Keep your tongue from evil and your lips from speaking deceit.

14 Turn away from evil and do good — seek peace and pursue it.

15 The eyes of YHWH are toward the righteous and his ears toward their cry.

16 The face of YHWH is against those who do evil, to cut off the memory of them from the earth.

17 When the righteous cry for help, YHWH hears and delivers them out of all their troubles.

18 YHWH is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit.

19 Many are the afflictions of the righteous, but YHWH delivers him out of them all.

20 He keeps all his bones — not one of them is broken.

21 Affliction will slay the wicked, and those who hate the righteous will be condemned.

22 YHWH redeems the life of his servants — none of those who take refuge in him will be condemned.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 34 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. It is an alphabetic acrostic. Verse 7 — the angel of YHWH encamps around those who fear him — connects directly to the War Scroll's theology of angelic protection. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 34 in full. Verse 8 — taste and see that YHWH is good — is read as the invitation to experiential covenant knowledge, not merely intellectual assent.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 34. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 18 — YHWH is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit. Verse 19 — many are the afflictions of the righteous, but YHWH delivers him out of them all. The afflictions of the righteous are real and many. The psalm does not minimize them. What it affirms is that YHWH delivers from all of them — not that they will not come. Verse 20 — he keeps all his bones — not one of them is broken. The complete preservation of the righteous person is YHWH's covenant commitment.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 35 Of Dawid — Contend, YHWH, With Those Who Contend With Me

1 Contend, YHWH, with those who contend with me — fight against those who fight against me.

2 Take hold of shield and buckler and rise for my help.

3 Draw the spear and javelin against my pursuers — say to my soul, I am your salvation.

4 Let them be put to shame and dishonor who seek after my life — let them be turned back and disappointed who devise evil against me.

5 Let them be like chaff before the wind, with the angel of YHWH driving them away.

6 Let their way be dark and slippery, with the angel of YHWH pursuing them.

7 For without cause they hid their net for me — without cause they dug a pit for my life.

8 Let destruction come upon him when he does not know it — and let the net that he hid ensnare him — let him fall into it to his destruction.

9 Then my soul will rejoice in YHWH, exulting in his salvation.

10 All my bones shall say, YHWH, who is like you, delivering the poor from him who is too strong for him, the poor and needy from him who robs him?

11 Malicious witnesses rise up — they ask me of things that I do not know.

12 They repay me evil for good — my soul is bereft.

13 But I, when they were sick — I wore sackcloth — I afflicted myself with fasting — I prayed with head bowed on my chest.

14 I went about as though I grieved for my friend or my brother — I bowed down heavily, as one who mourns for his mother.

15 But at my stumbling they rejoiced and gathered — they gathered together against me — wretches whom I did not know tore at me without ceasing.

16 Like profane mockers at a feast, they gnash at me with their teeth.

17 How long, YHWH, will you look on — rescue me from their destruction, my precious life from the lions.

18 I will thank you in the great congregation — in the mighty throng I will praise you.

19 Let not those rejoice over me who are wrongfully my foes, and let not those wink the eye who hate me without cause.

20 For they do not speak peace, but against those who are quiet in the land they devise words of deceit.

21 They open wide their mouths against me — they say, Aha, aha, our eyes have seen it.

22 You have seen, YHWH — be not silent — YHWH, be not far from me.

23 Awake and rouse yourself for my vindication, for my cause, my Creator and my Sovereign.

24 Vindicate me, YHWH my Creator, according to your righteousness, and let them not rejoice over me.

25 Let them not say in their hearts, Aha, our heart's desire — let them not say, We have swallowed him up.

26 Let them be put to shame and disappointed altogether who rejoice at my calamity — let them be clothed with shame and dishonor who magnify themselves against me.

27 Let those who delight in my righteousness shout for joy and be glad and say evermore, Great is YHWH, who delights in the welfare of his servant.

28 Then my tongue shall tell of your righteousness and of your praise all the day long.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 35 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm is an extensive lament over betrayal by former friends. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 35 in full. The detailed description of false witnesses and those who repay evil for good is read as the paradigmatic experience of the righteous person in a hostile social environment.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 35. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 13-14 — the psalmist mourned for those who are now his enemies when they were sick. He wore sackcloth, fasted, and prayed for them as for a friend, a brother, a mother. Their response to his stumbling is rejoicing and mockery. The contrast between his covenant loyalty to them in their trouble and their betrayal of him in his is the ethical heart of the psalm. Verse 27 — great is YHWH, who delights in the welfare of his servant. YHWH's own delight is the shalom — welfare, peace, completeness — of his servant.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 36 Of Dawid the Servant of YHWH — Transgression Speaks — The Fountain of Life

1 Transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart — there is no fear of Creator before his eyes.

2 For he flatters himself in his own eyes that his iniquity cannot be found out and hated.

3 The words of his mouth are trouble and deceit — he has ceased to act wisely and do good.

4 He plots trouble while on his bed — he sets himself in a way that is not good — he does not reject evil.

5 Your steadfast love, YHWH, extends to the heavens — your faithfulness to the clouds.

6 Your righteousness is like the mountains of Creator, your judgments are like the great deep — man and beast you save, YHWH.

7 How precious is your steadfast love, O Creator — the children of mankind take refuge in the shadow of your wings.

8 They feast on the abundance of your house, and you give them drink from the river of your delights.

9 For with you is the fountain of life — in your light do we see light.

10 Oh, continue your steadfast love to those who know you, and your righteousness to the upright of heart.

11 Let not the foot of arrogance come upon me, nor the hand of the wicked drive me away.

12 There the evildoers lie fallen — they are thrust down, unable to rise.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 36 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. Verse 1 uses the same Hebrew word for the inner counsel that Psalm 25:14 uses for YHWH's intimate counsel — the contrast is deliberate. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 36 in full. Verse 9 — with you is the fountain of life — in your light do we see light — is one of the most cited verses in the Beta Israel tradition as a declaration of YHWH as the ultimate source of all life and understanding.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 36. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — with you is the fountain of life — in your light do we see light. YHWH as the source of life itself, and YHWH as the source of illumination by which all other things are seen. The fountain of life is the covenant water imagery running through the entire Hebrew corpus — the living waters of YHWH contrasted with the broken cisterns of human making in Yirmeyahu 2:13. Verse 7 — the children of mankind take refuge in the shadow of your wings — echoes Psalms 17 and 57. The shadow of YHWH's wings is the recurring image of covenant protection.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 37 Of Dawid — An Acrostic — Fret Not — The Meek Shall Inherit the Land

1 Fret not yourself because of evildoers — be not envious of wrongdoers.

2 For they will soon fade like the grass and wither like the green herb.

3 Trust in YHWH, and do good — dwell in the land and befriend faithfulness.

4 Delight yourself in YHWH, and he will give you the desires of your heart.

5 Commit your way to YHWH — trust in him, and he will act.

6 He will bring forth your righteousness as the light, and your justice as the noonday.

7 Be still before YHWH and wait patiently for him — fret not yourself over the one who prospers in his way, over the man who carries out evil devices.

8 Refrain from anger, and forsake wrath — fret not yourself — it tends only to evil.

9 For the evildoers shall be cut off, but those who wait for YHWH shall inherit the land.

10 In just a little while, the wicked will be no more — though you look carefully at his place, he will not be there.

11 But the meek shall inherit the land and delight themselves in abundant peace.

12 The wicked plots against the righteous and gnashes his teeth at him,

13 But YHWH laughs at the wicked, for he sees that his day is coming.

14 The wicked draw the sword and bend their bows to bring down the poor and needy, to slay those whose way is upright.

15 Their sword shall enter their own heart, and their bows shall be broken.

16 Better is the little that the righteous has than the abundance of many wicked.

17 For the arms of the wicked shall be broken, but YHWH upholds the righteous.

18 YHWH knows the days of the blameless, and their heritage will remain forever.

19 They are not put to shame in evil times — in the days of famine they have abundance.

20 But the wicked will perish — the enemies of YHWH are like the glory of the pastures — they vanish — like smoke they vanish away.

21 The wicked borrows but does not pay back, but the righteous is generous and gives.

22 For those blessed by YHWH shall inherit the land, but those cursed by him shall be cut off.

23 The steps of a man are established by YHWH, when he delights in his way.

24 Though he fall, he shall not be cast headlong, for YHWH upholds his hand.

25 I have been young, and now am old, yet I have not seen the righteous forsaken or his children begging for bread.

26 He is ever lending generously, and his children become a blessing.

27 Turn away from evil and do good — so shall you dwell forever.

28 For YHWH loves justice — he will not forsake his saints. They are preserved forever, but the children of the wicked shall be cut off.

29 The righteous shall inherit the land and dwell upon it forever.

30 The mouth of the righteous utters wisdom, and his tongue speaks justice.

31 The Torah of his Creator is in his heart — his steps do not slip.

32 The wicked watches for the righteous and seeks to put him to death.

33 YHWH will not abandon him to his power or let him be condemned when he is brought to trial.

34 Wait for YHWH and keep his way, and he will exalt you to inherit the land — you will look on when the wicked are cut off.

35 I have seen a wicked, ruthless man, spreading himself like a green laurel tree.

36 But he passed away, and behold, he was no more — though I sought him, he could not be found.

37 Mark the blameless and behold the upright, for there is a future for the man of peace.

38 But transgressors shall be altogether destroyed — the future of the wicked shall be cut off.

39 The salvation of the righteous is from YHWH — he is their stronghold in the time of trouble.

40 YHWH helps them and delivers them — he delivers them from the wicked and saves them, because they take refuge in him.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 37 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. It is a substantial alphabetic acrostic — 40 verses. A pesher commentary on Psalm 37 was found at Qumran: 4Q171 (4QPesherPsalms). The Qumran community applied verse 11 — the meek shall inherit the land — directly to themselves as the covenant remnant. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 37 in full. The sustained meditation on the prosperity of the wicked and the patient waiting of the righteous is read as the covenant community's foundational posture in every generation of exile and oppression.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 37. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — the meek shall inherit the land and delight themselves in abundant peace. The 4QPesherPsalms commentary from Qumran applies this verse explicitly to the community of the poor — the anawim — who will inherit the land at the end of the period of wickedness. The meek are not the passive or the weak but those who have surrendered control of the outcome to YHWH while actively walking in his way. Verse 31 — the Torah of his Creator is in his heart — his steps do not slip. The internalized Torah is the foundation of the stable walk. Yirmeyahu 31:33 declares this as the new covenant promise. Psalm 37 shows it as the present reality of the covenant person.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 38 Of Dawid — A Psalm for the Memorial Offering — Do Not Rebuke Me in Wrath

1 YHWH, rebuke me not in your anger, nor discipline me in your wrath.

2 For your arrows have sunk into me, and your hand has come down on me.

3 There is no soundness in my flesh because of your indignation — there is no health in my bones because of my sin.

4 For my iniquities have gone over my head — like a heavy burden, they are too heavy for me.

5 My wounds stink and fester because of my foolishness.

6 I am utterly bowed down and prostrate — all the day I go about mourning.

7 For my sides are filled with burning, and there is no soundness in my flesh.

8 I am feeble and crushed — I groan because of the tumult of my heart.

9 YHWH, all my longing is before you — my sighing is not hidden from you.

10 My heart throbs — my strength fails me — and the light of my eyes — it also has gone from me.

11 My friends and companions stand aloof from my plague, and my nearest kin stand far off.

12 Those who seek my life lay their snares — those who seek my hurt speak of ruin and meditate treachery all day long.

13 But I am like a deaf man — I do not hear — like a mute man who does not open his mouth.

14 I have become like a man who does not hear, and in whose mouth are no rebukes.

15 But for you, YHWH, do I wait — it is you, YHWH my Creator, who will answer.

16 For I said, Only let them not rejoice over me, who boast against me when my foot slips.

17 For I am ready to fall, and my pain is ever before me.

18 I confess my iniquity — I am sorry for my sin.

19 But my foes are vigorous, they are mighty — and many are those who hate me wrongfully.

20 Those who render me evil for good accuse me because I follow after good.

21 Do not forsake me, YHWH — YHWH, be not far from me.

22 Make haste to help me, YHWH, my salvation.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 38 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions. Psalm 38 is the third of the traditional seven penitential psalms.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 38 in full. The abandonment by friends and companions while bearing a divinely given burden of discipline is read as one of the most painful experiences of the covenant life.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 38. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 13-14 — I am like a deaf man, like a mute man who does not open his mouth. The psalmist's silence before his accusers is not weakness but deliberate covenant posture — waiting for YHWH to vindicate rather than defending himself. Verse 15 — but for you, YHWH, do I wait. The silence before men and the waiting before YHWH are the same act. Verse 18 — I confess my iniquity — I am sorry for my sin. This is the confession of Psalm 32:5 repeated here in the context of continuing affliction. The confession does not produce immediate relief. But it is made. YHWH knows it. The waiting continues.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 39 Of Dawid — The Brevity of Life — I Am a Sojourner Like All My Fathers

1 I said, I will guard my ways, that I may not sin with my tongue — I will guard my mouth with a muzzle, so long as the wicked are in my presence.

2 I was mute and silent — I held my peace to no avail, and my distress grew worse.

3 My heart became hot within me — as I mused, the fire burned — then I spoke with my tongue.

4 YHWH, make me know my end and what is the measure of my days — let me know how fleeting I am.

5 Behold, you have made my days a few handbreadths, and my lifetime is as nothing before you — surely all mankind stands as a mere breath.

6 Surely a man goes about as a shadow — surely for nothing they are in turmoil — man heaps up wealth and does not know who will gather.

7 And now, YHWH, for what do I wait — my hope is in you.

8 Deliver me from all my transgressions — do not make me the scorn of the fool.

9 I am mute — I do not open my mouth, for it is you who have done it.

10 Remove your stroke from me — I am spent by the hostility of your hand.

11 When you discipline a man with rebukes for sin, you consume like a moth what is dear to him — surely all mankind is a mere breath.

12 Hear my prayer, YHWH, and give ear to my cry — hold not your peace at my tears — for I am a sojourner with you, a guest, like all my fathers.

13 Look away from me, that I may smile again, before I depart and am no more.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 39 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions. The meditation on human transience resonates deeply with the Qumran community's theology of the present age as temporary.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 39 in full. Verse 12 — I am a sojourner with you, a guest, like all my fathers — is read as the covenant community's fundamental self-understanding: they do not own the land; they are guests of YHWH in a land that is his.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 39. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — I am a sojourner with you, a guest, like all my fathers. The covenant people's relationship to the land is that of sojourners — ger and toshav — terms used of Avraham in Bereshit 23:4. The land belongs to YHWH. The people dwell in it as his guests. This theological status is the basis of the jubilee land law in Vayikra 25:23 — the land shall not be sold permanently, for the land is mine and you are sojourners and tenants with me. Psalm 39 applies this to existence itself: all of human life is a sojourn. Every generation is a guest.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 40 Of Dawid — I Waited Patiently — He Drew Me Up — Delight to Do Your Will

1 I waited patiently for YHWH — he inclined to me and heard my cry.

2 He drew me up from the pit of destruction, out of the miry bog, and set my feet upon a rock, making my steps secure.

3 He put a new song in my mouth, a song of praise to our Creator. Many will see and fear, and put their trust in YHWH.

4 Blessed is the man who makes YHWH his trust, who does not turn to the proud, to those who go astray after a lie.

5 You have multiplied, YHWH my Creator, your wondrous deeds and your thoughts toward us — none can compare with you. I will proclaim and tell of them, yet they are more than can be told.

6 In sacrifice and offering you have not delighted, but you have given me an open ear — burnt offering and sin offering you have not required.

7 Then I said, Behold, I have come — in the scroll of the book it is written of me.

8 I delight to do your will, O my Creator — your Torah is within my heart.

9 I have told the glad news of deliverance in the great congregation — behold, I have not restrained my lips, as you know, YHWH.

10 I have not hidden your deliverance within my heart — I have spoken of your faithfulness and your salvation — I have not concealed your steadfast love and your faithfulness from the great congregation.

11 As for you, YHWH, you will not restrain your mercy from me — your steadfast love and your faithfulness will ever preserve me.

12 For evils have encompassed me beyond number — my iniquities have overtaken me, and I cannot see — they are more than the hairs of my head — my heart fails me.

13 Be pleased, YHWH, to deliver me — YHWH, make haste to help me.

14 Let those be put to shame and disappointed altogether who seek to snatch away my life — let those be turned back and brought to dishonor who delight in my hurt.

15 Let those be appalled because of their shame who say to me, Aha, aha.

16 But may all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you — may those who love your salvation say continually, Great is YHWH.

17 As for me, I am poor and needy, but YHWH takes thought for me. You are my help and my deliverer — do not delay, YHWH, my Creator.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 40 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. Verses 6-8 — in sacrifice and offering you have not delighted — your Torah is within my heart — are among the most theologically significant verses in the Tehillim for the Qumran community, whose theology of interior Torah observance aligns precisely with this passage. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 40 in full. The psalm is read as the complete covenant testimony — rescue from destruction, the new song given, the Torah internalized, and the ongoing cry from poverty and need.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 40. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — I delight to do your will, O my Creator — your Torah is within my heart. This is the fulfillment of the new covenant promise of Yirmeyahu 31:33 — I will put my Torah within them and write it on their hearts. Verses 6-8 together declare that external sacrifice is insufficient — what YHWH requires is the open ear — the obedient heart that hears — and the internalized Torah. This is the theology of the Qumran community's understanding of their prayer and study as a legitimate substitute for the temple sacrifices during their period of separation.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 41 Of Dawid — Blessed Is the One Who Considers the Poor — Closing Doxology of Book I

1 Blessed is the one who considers the poor — in the day of trouble YHWH delivers him.

2 YHWH protects him and keeps him alive — he is called blessed in the land — you do not give him up to the will of his enemies.

3 YHWH sustains him on his sickbed — in his illness you restore him to full health.

4 As for me, I said, YHWH, be gracious to me — heal my soul, for I have sinned against you.

5 My enemies say of me in malice, When will he die, and his name perish?

6 And when one comes to see me, he utters empty words, while his heart gathers iniquity — when he goes out, he tells it abroad.

7 All who hate me whisper together about me — they imagine the worst for me.

8 They say, A deadly thing is poured out on him — he will not rise again from where he lies.

9 Even my close friend in whom I trusted, who ate my bread, has lifted his heel against me.

10 But you, YHWH, be gracious to me, and raise me up, that I may repay them.

11 By this I know that you delight in me — my enemy will not shout in triumph over me.

12 But you have upheld me because of my integrity, and set me in your presence forever.

13 Blessed be YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, from everlasting to everlasting — Amen and Amen.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 41 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. Verse 9 — even my close friend in whom I trusted, who ate my bread, has lifted his heel against me — is cited in the Qumran Damascus Document in the context of betrayal within the covenant community. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 41 in full. Verse 13 — Blessed be YHWH the Creator of Yisra'EL from everlasting to everlasting, Amen and Amen — is the doxology that closes Book I of the Tehillim in both the Masoretic and the Orit Ge'ez traditions.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 41. Verse 13 is the editorial doxology that closes Book I of the Masoretic Tehillim. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 13 — Amen and Amen — the double Amen that closes Book I is the covenant seal of the entire first book of the Tehillim. The double affirmation is the liturgical declaration that everything in the book is true and binding. The same double and triple Amen appears at the end of the War Scroll Column XIX. Psalm 41 closes Book I with the themes that opened it in Psalm 1: the blessed man, the covenant relationship with YHWH, and the contrast between the righteous who are upheld and the wicked who are not. Book I begins and ends with blessing.

END OF PART TWO — PSALMS 21 THROUGH 41 — BOOK I COMPLETE
